2. Frequentist Inference

Three goals of inference:

  1. Estimation
  2. Confidence Intervals
  3. Hypothesis Tests

These are all procedures: given that data we compute a statistic and infer something.

Definition 6. Let be a sequence of i.i.d. random variables with common distribution .

A statistical model is a pair where

  • is the sample space,
  • is the family of probability distributions on , and
  • is the parameter set.

We assume the statistical model is specified, i.e. for some .

Given a statistical model and observed data, the likelihood function expresses how plausiable each makes the observed data:

(30)

This is the same definition that Bayesians use, except they care about the posterior: .

2.1. Point Estimation

Definition 7.

  • is identified if the map is injective.
  • A statistic is a measurable function of the sample.
  • An estimator of is any statistic whose expression does not depend on .

  • An estimator of is consistent if

    (31)

    That is, as the sample size increases, the probability that the estimator differs from the true value by goes to zero goes to zero.

Definition 8. Let denote expectation with respect to the distribution of the data when the distribution has parameter , i.e.

The bias of an estimator is the vector

(32)

The variance / covariance matrix of an estimator is

(33)

A closely related definition is the total variance:

(34)

The risk of an estimator is

(35)

Note that an estimator being unbiased does not imply consistency and vice versa.

  • The biased sample variance is consistent ().
  • is an unbiased estimate for the mean but is obviously not consistent.

Proposition 8 (Bias Variance Decomposition). In 1D, risk equals bias squared plus variance. In general, we have

(36)

Bias usually decreases with model flexibility (less underfitting). Variance usually increases (overfitting).

In terms of model fitting, we have a family of estimators indexed by a parameter , e.g. regularization or polynomial degree. Then bias and variance and risk depend on . This works if the model is the same for all (e.g. in ridge regression we still get a slope). But for a more general viewpoint, we switch from parameter-estimation viewpoint to function-estimation viewpoint. If we predict instead of , we can measure bias, risk, and variance as a function of model complexity. [REVIEW] See 677 notes for notion of irreducible error.

where is the degree of the polynomial.

The log-likelihood for is a sum of independent points. So by Lindeberg CLT it’s Gaussian with variance proportional to . Take a taylor series to compute the variance.

Definition 9 (Fisher Information). Let be the likelihood function, i.e. is distributed according to .

(41)

Theorem 7 (Cramer–Rao Bound). For any unbiased estimator , under regularity conditions

(42)

In multiple dimensions, we have

(43)

where means is positive semidefinite. This is a stronger statement than the scalar bound on the total variance:

(44)

Intuitively, the Fisher information is related to the curvature of the likelihood function near , and thus sharper peaks lead to more precision.

Definition 10. We say an unbiased estimator is efficient if it saturates the CR bound. Biased estimators by definition cannot be efficient.

A more general definition of efficiency that applies for consistent estimators is the variance of an estimator relative to the CR bound to another estimator, since usually the estimators are consistent and asymptotically unbiased. The relative efficiency is then the ratio of their variances.

We can beat the CR bound if regularity conditions aren’t satisfied or with unbiased estimators (for example: James-Stein estimator for the mean).

Example 7. Estimating the mean of a Gaussian distribution with known based on points. See Notability
Example 8. Under the classical linear regression assumptions, the OLS attains the Cramer-Rao bound for , by Gauss-Markov.
Example 9. The joint fit MLE for of a Gaussian is actually just the individual MLEs and the biased sample variance. The partial derivative equaions are decoupled so we can solve the individual cases.

2.2. Robust Estimators

For i.i.d. , the variance of the mean is . The variance of the median is ([TODO] how? median is computed using empirical cdf). The ratio is , and we say the sample median is 64% efficienct compared to the mean (only uses of the information compared to the mean under Gaussian assumptions, Definition 10).

But under heavy-tailed data, the mean’s variance can blow up and the median becomes more efficient.

We can assess robustness as a function of how misspecified our model is. One metric is the breakdown point: fraction of contaminated data needed to drive the estimator arbitrarily wrong. Mean is 0%. Median is 50%. Another is sensitivity to small outliers.

Generally we have an efficiency-robustness tradeoff. Robust procedures often have lower efficiency under the ideal model, but they degrade more gracefully under misspecification.

2.3. Confidence Intervals

Definition 11. Let . A confidence interval of level for is any random interval whose boundaries do not depend on and

(45)

Think of as a procedure. For any true value of , if we repeatedly sample from and contsruct the CI, we include at least of the time.

To construct a confidence interval:

  1. Find an estimator for the parameter with nice sampling distributions (or asymptotics if we only care about large ).

  2. Pick the appropriate quantiles from the sampling distribution.

    • If the estimator is biased, try to make it unbiased. Easy in the case where bias is known to be some constant offset/multiplicative factor. Actually this might only be useful if its gaussian.
    • Or just use the sampling distribution (e.g. Example 11)
Example 10 (CI for of a Gaussian). Suppose we have i.i.d. measurements from a Gaussian and want to estimate .

If is known, we can construct our CI from the fact that

If is unknown, we can get a similar expression from using the sample variance. In particular we invoke the following two magical facts about the sample variance for Gaussian in Section 1.3.3:

  1. .
  2. and are independent.

Example 11 (Estimating maximum of uniform distribution). See notability.
Example 12 (CI for in Binomial). The standard error of depends on ! Uh oh! The naive Wald CI does not guarantee the precise coverage property. Getting exactly coverage requires randomization. [TODO] We can get confidence interval with using Clopper Pearson.

2.4. Hypothesis Tests

Like confidence intervals, hypothesis tests are a procedure that, in the long run, makes only a controlled fraction of mistakes ( and ).

Assume the model is well-specified and split the parameter space into disjoint, complementary subsets defined by and .

Definition 12. A hypothesis test at level is a random decision rule that outputs or .

  • : “fail to reject “
  • : “reject “

If is true, the procedure satisfies

(46)

This means that no matter which in the null region is the true one, the probability of false rejection never exceeds .

The power of the test is . The Type II error is when .

The coverage is only for false rejection (Type I error). We don’t have a similar coverage guarantee for . Ideally we design our test to have a small .

Why not flip and ? By construction our test controls the false rejection rate. Hence we want to set to be disease presence. False negative is way worse than false positive. Similiarly, in scientific discoveries, we set “no effect” as the null to avoid spurious discoveries.

Definition 13. The -value is defined to be

(47)

If is not a point hypothesis, then we choose the worst as mentioned above.

Generally we construct tests of the form

(48)

Example 13.

(49)

We compute the test statistic at because it gives the largest p-value.

Proposition 9. A two-sided hypothesis test at level for will reject if and only if is not contained in the confidence interval for .

Example 14 (Common Tests).

  • Student’s/Welch’s t-test: test whether difference in groups is statistically significant, when they have the same/different unknown population variances.

  • and KS: goodness of fit

    • Shapiro-Wilk is testing for normality
  • F-test and Levene’s (more robust) test: assess equality of variances of two distributions.

  • Regression

    • Linreg test for slope: use sampling distribution for beat

    • F-test: Does a linear model fit better than a flat mean?

      • More generally, the F-test is for deciding whether to reject a smaller reduced model in favor of the larger model (e.g. flat versus line, line versus quadratic etc)
  • ANOVA: Suppose we have groups of data, each group sampled from a distribution with mean . The null is that all means are equal, and the alternative is that at least one mean differs.

  • Likelihood Ratio Test: compare best fit under null (maximum likelihood) to best fit overall. If the ratio is small, the null fits much worse than the full model. The distribution for the ratio statistic turns out to follow a .

    • The classical tests are all special cases of the likelihood ratio test.
    • By Neyman–Pearson, LR test has the most power compared to other tests for fixed .

We dedicate the next section to two important tests.

2.5. Chi-Squared and KS Tests

The -distribution with degrees of freedom is the distribution of the sum of squares of independent standard normal random variables.

These pop up in tests used for

  1. Goodness-of-fit (does this dice look fair)
  2. Test of independence (are two categorical variables independent?)

2.5.1. Goodness of Fit

Say you have discrete outcomes or bins from a continuous distribution. Then for each bin, we should have with . In fact, the vector is sampled from a multinomial with and for .2

As , by the multinomial CLT we have

(50)

Proposition: has rank . The null space is spanned by .

Define the chi-squared statistic as:

(51)

where and Let , so that from Lemma 1. Then .

Proposition: After some simplification, we get where , which is the orthogonal projection onto the -dimensional subspace perpendicular to . Rotating coordinates yields .

The intuition for the dof is as follows: if you know through , is uniquely determined by subtracting from . So the randomness comes from taking variables.

Summary: We have found a nice statistic to use for our test. The rule of thumb for CLT to apply is that .

Remarks:

When CLT doesn’t apply, we use Fisher’s exact test.

The binless continuous analog is the Kolmogorov–Smirnov / KS test, which answers

  1. Does a sample come from a fully specifide continuous cdf ?
  2. Do two samples come from the same (unknown) continuous distribution?

The statistic is the largest vertical gap between their CDF curves which follows a Kolmogorov distribution. The vertical gap is a Brownian bridge (Brownian motion conditioned on that the process has the same value at and ).

2.5.2. Independence Test

Suppose we have two categorical variables and with and levels respectively. Then we can apply a gof test to the joint pdf to check for independence:

(52)

The twist is that we assume we don’t know the marginals in advance, so we estimate them from the data. This reduces the dof from to . This pops out of the multivariate normal approximations but is hard to see. (Fisher and Pearson had a feud about it).

If we did know the hypothesized marginals, then we’d be back to an ordinary gof test with dof.

2.6. Resampling Methods (e.g. Bootstrapping)

Assessing estimators, confidence intervals, hypothesis tests require us to think about repeatedly sampling new datasets from the true distribution. Since we can’t do this, we instead try resampling from the data itself.

  1. Bootstrapping: Resample with replacement from our data, same sample size . Compute statistic on each sample to get a distribution for .

    • Motivation: Obviously this works for large . Hence its worth trying in general.

  2. Jackknife: Systematically leave one observation out, recompute the statistic everytime.

    • Motivation: If your estimate is stable to leaving out one observation, then its variance is low.
  3. Permutation tests: If these two variables are unrelated, then we can randomly permute the labels and recompute the statistic each time to get a distribution under and then compute a p-value.
  4. Cross Validation: Duh

2.7. Model selection criterion (AIC, BIC, CV)

The goal is to balance fit vs complexity. Let be the number of parameters.

(53)

  • AIC and CV are good if you care about minimizing prediction error. CV is nonparametric too.
  • BIC is good if you believe there is a correct model, as it is asymptotically consistent (selects the true model if it’s in the candidate set).
  • AIC is motivated by minimizing KL divergence (information theory).

  • BIC comes from minimizing the Bayesian marginal likelihood

    (54)

    Recall a model specifies both a form on the likelihood and a prior on the parameters. We assume the likelihood is peaked around the MLE (this happens for large ) and approximate the integral using Laplace’s method. The prior actually cancels out this way!

  • CV operationalizes the definition of risk and is used in ML contexts.
